result:
The first thing that strikes me is the way light pools on the kitchen floor at 3:17 p.m., a slow tide of gold that makes the old linoleum look almost sacred. I am barefoot, always barefoot in the house, because shoes are for people who intend to leave. The refrigerator hums in the key of C. I checked once with a guitar tuner: the compressor vibrates at 130.8 hertz, give or take the shiver of electricity in the walls. My mother used to say the house sings when it thinks no one is listening. She died before I learned the chords.

There is a fly bumping against the window, repeating its tiny head-butt mantra. I could open the screen and let it out, but instead I watch the shadow it casts—an inkblot Morse code on the sill. Everything is language if you stare long enough. The coffee ring on yesterday’s newspaper is a nebula; the crack in the plaster is a lightning bolt frozen mid-throe. I once loved a woman who read fortunes from the veins in leaves. She held each blade to the sun, claimed the branching red rivers told her when the world would end. It ended, of course, on a Tuesday. We had pancakes. She left the maple syrup out, and now it’s crystallized into a landscape of tiny amber glaciers. I can’t bring myself to throw it away.

I keep thinking about the word threshold. The plank beneath the door you cross, yes, but also the liminal voltage where a neuron decides to fire, the decimal at which pain becomes interesting. When I was nine I stepped on a nail—rusty, thick as a drinking straw—and stood there surprised by how little it hurt. The blood was warmer than I expected, almost syrupy. My father carried me to the car, my foot cupped in his palm like a wounded bird. His hands smelled of turpentine and the metallic sweetness of sawdust. In the emergency room I watched the nurse thread the suture needle through my skin, a tiny silver fish leaping in and out of a pink lake. She asked if I wanted to keep the nail. I said yes. It lived in a shoebox under my bed for years, rust blooming across it like slow fireworks. One day it was gone. I like to imagine it returned to the earth, atom by atom, until it became the color of a cloud before rain.

Memory is mostly holes. Scientists call this forgetting curve, as if the past slides downhill and we can measure its acceleration. I picture it more like Swiss cheese left out in the sun: the edges sag, the holes widen, eventually you’re holding a lace of milk. Still, some moments stay pegged to the brain’s clothesline with clothespins of adrenaline or shame. The first time I saw snow—real snow, not the TV-static flurries of my southern childhood—I was twenty-three and drunk on someone’s cheap vodka in a Boston apartment. I stepped outside in a T-shirt, barefoot again, and the sidewalk was a blank page. I wrote my name with an icicle. It looked like someone else’s handwriting.

They say the universe is flat, like a sheet of paper stretched to infinity. I don’t buy it. The universe is a crumpled ball in the coat pocket of a teenager who forgot to do the laundry. Inside: gum wrappers, a faded ticket to a concert that no longer exists, the smell of rain on denim. Sometimes I reach in and feel around for the corner of something vast. What I pull out is usually a receipt for gas station coffee or the business card of a locksmith I called at 2 a.m. when I locked myself out of my own cliché. The card is soft at the edges, like it’s been trying to become cloth.

Once, I tried to catalog every place I’ve cried. Bathrooms are obvious—airports, workplaces, the ivy-walled stall of a museum where I learned my favorite painter had died young. Less obvious: the cereal aisle (the sudden sight of Count Chocula resurrected a campout with my brother, dead now eight years), the parking garage level G4 where the echo made my sobs sound operatic, the laundromat when someone else’s red sock bled into my only white shirt and I realized I was looking at my heart. The list grew so long it became a second skin. I imagined shedding it like a snake, leaving the translucent sheath of grief curled on the trail. A hiker would find it, hold it to the light, see the stained glass of every salt-stained minute.

I read that trees communicate through their roots, trading sugar for phosphorus in a slow underground economy. When one is sick, the others send extra food. They keep the stump alive long after it can no longer leaf, a botanical ghost supported by its communal IV. I wonder if humans do this in reverse: we sicken each other with stories, withdraw nutrients with silence. My friend hasn’t answered my texts in three weeks. I keep typing sentences, deleting them, as if the right arrangement of pixels could photosynthesize into hope. Yesterday I sent a single 🌳 emoji. He replied with a thumbs-up. Progress, maybe. Or just polite forestry.

Tonight the moon is a clipped fingernail, small and careless, as if God were a nail-biter. I stand outside and let the dew soak my ankles. Somewhere a dog barks the first three notes of “My Heart Will Go On.” I join in on the fourth, a minor sixth that wavers like heat. The porch light attracts moths whose wings are maps of places they will never reach. I cup one gently, feel its panic vibrate against my lifeline. Release is easy; keeping it alive is the hard part. It flutters off, a gray confetti scrap, and I pretend it carries a piece of my shadow. Tomorrow I will check the news for unexplained dark spots over the Midwest.

Inside again, the clock divides the night into clicks. I remember that as a child I thought time was a ladder you climbed upward, rung by rung, until you reached the attic of adulthood where everything was stored in labeled boxes. Instead it’s more like an escalator going down, and you’re trying to walk upward while people pass you carrying bags you don’t recognize. Eventually you realize the only way to win is to sit, let the metal stairs ferry you into fluorescent-lit parking garage of the future, where your car is hopefully still waiting, keys hidden above the rear tire, engine idling in neutral, radio playing a song you almost remember learning the words to.
